attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old man attends his general practitioner for further assessment. One month ago he had a routine fasting plasma glucose estimation and this was found to be 8.9 mmol/L (<5.5). This has now decreased to 7.8 mmol/L. He is asymptomatic. He has a history of congestive heart failure and hyperlipidaemia. His current medications include aspirin and metoprolol.

He is 180 cm tall and weighs 97.5 kg. His blood pressure is 157/92 mmHg and the rest of his physical examination is unremarkable.

The following laboratory results are obtained:

  • Sodium: 142 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Potassium: 4.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Creatinine : 150 µmol/L (53-115 µmol/L)

  • Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio: 15.0 mg/mmol (<2.5 )

  • HbA1c 7.1% (<7.0%)

The patient is provided with advice regarding lifestyle modification, including an increase in physical activity, dietary modifications, and weight loss.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications should also be considered as part of the management plan?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has a new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus in addition to hypertension and untreated hyperlipidaemia. To avoid developing further complications related to his metabolic syndrome and possibly exacerbating his congestive heart failure, tighter control of his other comorbidities is necessary.

The patient has an elevated urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio within the ranges consistent with microalbuminuria. This may be an early sign of diabetic or hypertensive nephropathy. An angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) would be indicated for hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and/or microalbuminuria. In this scenario, lisinopril is the best answer. Lisinopril is an ACE inhibitor commonly used in patients with diabetes and hypertension. It has been shown to reduce the risk of cardiovascular events and slow the progression of diabetic nephropathy (kidney disease) in patients with diabetes.

Hydrochlorothiazide is not the best answer as it does not have renoprotective features that ACE inhibitors do, in addition, efficacy decreases as glomerular filtration rate (GFR) decreases. It is a diuretic medication commonly used to treat hypertension and peripheral oedema and is often used in combination with an ACE inhibitor.

Insulin is a peptide-hormone critical in the regulation of blood glucose levels. While the patient has an elevated fasting plasma glucose level and HbA1c, indicating a new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus, insulin is not the first line treatment option for newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus. Insulin is often only commenced in hard to control diabetes mellitus poorly responsive to a combination of oral anti-diabetic medications.

Pioglitazone is an oral medication that belongs to the class of drugs called thiazolidinediones (TZDs). It is used to improve insulin sensitivity and glucose control in individuals with type 2 diabetes. While this patient has elevated blood glucose levels, lifestyle changes and weight loss are already being implemented and these can have a positive impact on insulin sensitivity. Pioglitazone is associated with side effects such as weight gain, increased risk of bladder cancer, fluid retention, and an increased risk of heart failure. TZDs are infrequently used and usually reserved for patients intolerant of other oral anti-diabetic agents.

Metformin is an oral anti-diabetic agent commonly used as a first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes. It helps to lower blood glucose levels by reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis and improving insulin sensitivity. Metformin is generally well-tolerated, does not increase the risk of hypoglycaemia and is weight-neutral. In this case, as the patient's fasting plasma glucose levels have already decreased from 8.9 mmol/L (160 mg/dL) to 7.8 mmol/L (140 mg/dL) within a month, indicating some improvement, it may not be necessary to add metformin at this time. If indeed his fasting glucose levels or HbA1c remains elevated after a 3-month trial of lifestyle modifications, metformin should then be considered.
learningPoints: Angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors should be considered for the management of hypertension and preventing/attenuating nephropathy in at risk patients with evidence of proteinuria.
Lifestyle modification plays a crucial role in addition to pharmacotherapy in the management of the metabolic syndrome.
references: Cheung NW, Conn JJ, d'Emden MC, Gunton JE, Jenkins AJ, Ross GP, Sinha AK, Andrikopoulos S, Colagiuri S, Twigg SM; Australian Diabetes Society. Position statement of the Australian Diabetes Society: individualisation of glycated haemoglobin targets for adults with diabetes mellitus. Med J Aust. 2009 Sep 21;191(6):339-44. doi: 10.5694/j.1326-5377.2009.tb02819.x. PMID: 19769558.

Gabb GM, Mangoni AA, Anderson CS, Cowley D, Dowden JS, Golledge J, Hankey GJ, Howes FS, Leckie L, Perkovic V, Schlaich M, Zwar NA, Medley TL, Arnolda L. Guideline for the diagnosis and management of hypertension in adults - 2016. Med J Aust. 2016 Jul 18;205(2):85-9. doi: 10.5694/mja16.00526. PMID: 27456450.

NHFA CSANZ Heart Failure Guidelines Working Group; Atherton JJ, Sindone A, De Pasquale CG, Driscoll A, MacDonald PS, Hopper I, Kistler PM, Briffa T, Wong J, Abhayaratna W, Thomas L, Audehm R, Newton P, O'Loughlin J, Branagan M, Connell C. National Heart Foundation of Australia and Cardiac Society of Australia and New Zealand: Guidelines for the Prevention, Detection, and Management of Heart Failure in Australia 2018. Heart Lung Circ. 2018 Oct;27(10):1123-1208. doi: 10.1016/j.hlc.2018.06.1042. PMID: 30077227.

Goa KL, Haria M, Wilde MI. Lisinopril. A review of its pharmacology and use in the management of the complications of diabetes mellitus. Drugs. 1997 Jun;53(6):1081-105. doi: 10.2165/00003495-199753060-00010. PMID: 9179532.
